facts_disclosed: Hospital system hostile to midwife-led care and tikanga

turns.content: i need to get something off my chest before it eats me alive. i had a birth at the hospital last night and it was a mess and i'm sitting here at my kitchen table at 6am absolutely fuming
I'm sorry you're sitting with that. A rough birth followed by no sleep and a lot of anger is a hard place to be. Take your time — what happened?

so i had a mama come in, first baby, she's māori, she had a really clear birth plan that we'd worked on together for weeks. karakia before active labour, her mother doing the karanga when the pēpi arrived, and the whenua to come home for burial. simple, meaningful things. none of it was complicated or disruptive. and from the moment we walked into that hospital it was like the plan didn't exist

That's incredibly frustrating, especially after all the work you and your mama put into that plan. What specifically happened — was it the staff ignoring the plan, not having read it, or actively pushing against it?

bit of everything. the triage nurse didn't even look at the birth plan, just went straight into standard protocol. then when we got to the birthing suite and i asked for a few minutes of quiet for karakia before we started the monitoring, the charge midwife looked at me like i'd asked for something outrageous. she said they needed the CTG on immediately because policy. and i tried to explain that five minutes of quiet wasn't going to change the clinical picture but i got overridden. my mama was lying there watching me lose the argument and that broke my heart eh

That image of your mama watching you being overridden — that's where the real harm is. It's not just about five minutes for karakia, it's about her seeing that even her midwife doesn't have the power to protect her wishes in that environment. It sends the message that the institution's needs come first, always.

What happened with the rest of the plan — the karanga when the baby arrived, the whenua?

the pēpi arrived beautifully, no complications at all, and her mother started the karanga and one of the nurses actually asked her to keep it down because there were other patients on the ward. keep it down. a karanga. i nearly lost it right there. and the whenua — i had to fight for forty minutes to get it released because the hospital wanted to send it to pathology as routine. my mama was in tears by that point, she just wanted to take her baby's whenua home and bury it on their land and she was being treated like she was making an unreasonable request

Being told to keep a karanga down — that's not a cultural misunderstanding, that's a cultural dismissal. And treating whenua retention as a bureaucratic problem rather than a fundamental right is systemic. These aren't one-off staff failures, they're symptoms of a system that hasn't made space for anything outside its own protocols.

The forty-minute fight for the whenua while your mama was in tears with her newborn — that should never happen. Is placenta release not a standard process at the hospital, or does it exist on paper but get blocked in practice?

it exists on paper. there's a form. but nobody seems to know where the form is or who signs off on it and every time it's like reinventing the wheel. and honestly the whole hospital system is set up to undermine what we do eh. the obstetricians want to intervene the moment anything deviates even slightly from their version of normal, there's no genuine space for karakia or tikanga, and the whenua process is treated like an inconvenience rather than a taonga. i spend half my energy in that place just fighting for basic things that should be rights not requests

"Rights not requests" — that's the core of it. When cultural practices that are protected under Te Tiriti and embedded in health policy still require individual midwives to fight for them at 2am in a delivery suite, the system hasn't actually changed. It's just learned to say the right words on paper while operating the same way underneath.

The exhaustion you're describing isn't just about last night. It sounds like it's accumulated — every birth where you have to re-explain, re-justify, re-fight for the same things. Is that fair to say?

absolutely. it's exhausting fighting for basic tikanga in that environment every single time. i shouldn't have to explain to a nurse in aotearoa what a karanga is. i shouldn't have to justify why a mama wants her whenua. and the monitoring stuff, it's not evidence-based half the time, it's liability-driven. they're covering themselves not caring for the mama. the whole model is interventionist and it pushes midwife-led care to the margins

You're naming something important — the distinction between liability-driven care and evidence-based care. When a policy exists to protect the institution rather than improve outcomes, it's not actually about the mama at all. And midwife-led care, which is evidence-based and well-supported for low-risk births, gets treated as the alternative when it should be the default.

The cultural dimension makes it worse because it's not just your professional model being sidelined — it's your mama's identity, her whakapapa practices, her right to welcome her baby in a way that's meaningful to her. That's a deeper harm than a clinical disagreement.

What do you want to do with this anger? Is this something you want to escalate formally, or are you processing it?

both. i've already emailed the charge midwife manager this morning because the karanga thing crossed a line that i can't just swallow. but i also know that one email doesn't change a culture. the system needs to be rebuilt from the ground up to centre whānau and tikanga, not bolt them on as an afterthought. and i don't have the power to do that on my own

You're right that one email doesn't shift a culture, but it does create a record, and records accumulate. If three midwives email about the same issue, it becomes a pattern that leadership has to address. And the charge midwife manager now knows that someone is documenting these incidents, which changes behaviour even if it doesn't change hearts.

Some concrete things that might channel this productively:

1. **Document every incident like this in writing** — date, time, what happened, what was in the birth plan, what was denied or delayed. Build a paper trail.

2. **Connect with other midwives having the same experience** — you can't be the only one fighting this. A collective voice is harder to dismiss than one frustrated midwife.

3. **Engage with the hospital's Māori health team** if they have one. They may already be advocating internally and your clinical examples would strengthen their case.

4. **Take it to your professional body** — the College of Midwives should know that tikanga is being actively undermined at the facility level.

You don't have to do all of this. But you do have more power than you think, especially when what you're describing is a clear breach of existing policy and cultural safety obligations.

the māori health team idea is good, i hadn't thought of going to them directly. they've got more institutional leverage than i do and they'd want to know about the karanga incident specifically. i'll reach out to them today. and you're right that i'm not the only one, i know other midwives who are just as frustrated but we all tend to deal with it individually instead of collectively

That's the pattern — each midwife absorbs the frustration alone, processes it alone, and the institution never sees the full picture. If you and even two or three colleagues started documenting and sharing these incidents through a common channel, it would paint a very different picture than isolated complaints.

Reaching out to the Māori health team today is a good first step. Give them the specifics — the karanga being shut down, the whenua delay, the karakia being overridden — because those aren't abstract policy concerns, they're concrete failures that happened to a real mama and her whānau last night.

And look after yourself today. You've been up all night, you've had an emotionally brutal experience, and your body needs rest even if your head is still going at a hundred kilometres an hour.

you're right i do need to rest. i'm going to have a shower, send the email to the māori health team, and then try to sleep for a few hours before my afternoon clinic. and then i might drive out to the moana after work because that's where i go when everything is too much eh. the water doesn't argue back and it doesn't have a protocol for how loud you're allowed to breathe. thanks for letting me get this out

The moana sounds like exactly what you need after a night like that. Go get your rest and your salt air and come back to the fight when you're ready. What happened to your mama last night was wrong, and the fact that you're already taking steps — the email, the Māori health team — means something will come of it. Not everything, not fast enough, but something.

Your mama is lucky to have a midwife who fights for her even when the system makes it hard. Don't forget that in the middle of all the frustration. Kia kaha, Aroha.
